does: This paper introduces RECAP, an agentic pipeline that systematically extracts memorized training data from large language models through iterative refinement. The method employs a feedback loop where a secondary language model evaluates initial extraction attempts, identifies discrepancies against reference passages, and generates minimal correction hints to guide the target model toward accurate reproduction. RECAP also incorporates a jailbreaking module to overcome alignment-induced refusals that prevent the model from producing certain content. Evaluation on EchoTrace, a benchmark comprising over 30 full books, demonstrates substantial improvements over single-pass extraction methods, with GPT-4.1 achieving a ROUGE-L score of 0.47 compared to 0.38 for baseline approaches.
connects: This work builds on the emerging research area of understanding LLM memorization and training data leakage, which has become increasingly important for understanding model behavior and privacy implications. It extends prior work on extracting memorized content through prompting strategies and adversarial approaches by introducing a more sophisticated multi-turn framework with verification and correction mechanisms. The paper relates to broader literature on model interpretability, understanding what language models have learned, and methods for detecting when models reproduce training data verbatim. It also connects to research on jailbreaking and adversarial prompting techniques that overcome safety-aligned model refusals, demonstrating how such methods can be applied systematically within a structured extraction pipeline.
enables: This work opens new directions for auditing and transparency around training data in large language models, particularly for copyrighted content. The systematic extraction approach could facilitate legal discovery processes, copyright infringement investigations, and regulatory compliance checking of LLM providers. The agentic framework itself demonstrates how iterative refinement and multi-model verification can improve extraction fidelity, potentially inspiring similar approaches for other information recovery tasks from black-box models. Future work could extend this methodology to other sensitive or protected information categories, develop more sophisticated jailbreaking techniques, and create better defenses against such extraction, contributing to an arms race in model transparency and security.
